metadata.title: Claude, Judged
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: Audience-namespaced adaptation. Facts and visual argument preserved from the canonical reel. B08 states when to use and not use AI; B09 is the audience outro.

beats.narration_text: Can a machine tell us whether a video is good? It can measure the file, count the beats, and test every declared rule. Yet the question we care about remains. Is this worth another person's attention? The interesting boundary begins exactly there.

props.command: can you tell me if this video is good?

beats.narration_text: Start with the smallest possible gate. Before generating audio, the system looks for one line: VERDICT: PASS. It does not understand the whole film. It answers one narrow question with complete reliability. There is something beautiful in giving a test no more authority than it has earned.

props.title: GATE P — generate_audio.py
props.code:
ped = Path(slug_dir) / 'PEDAGOGY.md'
if not re.search(
    r'^\s*VERDICT:\s*PASS\b', ped.read_text()
):
    print('[err] GATE P: no VERDICT: PASS')
    sys.exit(3)
# cheap. deterministic. correct.

beats.narration_text: Now two kinds of questions appear. Some are determinable: does the schema validate, do nine beats exist, is the file where it should be? Others are judgeable: is the idea alive, is the pacing humane, should this be published? The first kind becomes a test. The second remains a responsibility.

A cheap gate protects the expensive act of judgment. It can stop a bad plan before voices are generated and hours are spent. But it cannot watch on our behalf. The final cut still has to meet a person who can notice boredom, confusion, delight, and consequence.

An agent can inspect the things that leave evidence: fields, durations, paths, resolution, and QC frames. These are not lesser tasks. They are the structure that lets attention move toward the harder question instead of being wasted on preventable mistakes.

What should never disappear into automation is the decision that carries meaning: whether the work is good, whether it earns the viewer's time, whether its simplifications are fair, whether it should leave the workshop. The agent can inform that decision. It cannot inherit it.

The series keeps returning to the same architecture. Route models by cost. Expand trust only after evidence. Freeze hard-won judgment into skills. Pin exact instructions when variation is dangerous. Treat outputs as distributions. Condition them deliberately. Then ask who signs. Each move places uncertainty where it can be seen.

So the boundary is not human versus machine. It is evidence versus judgment. Let machines check what can be demonstrated: schema, timing, paths, resolution. Let humans decide what must be answered for: interest, care, and publication. The most capable system may be the one that knows where its capability ends.

artifactLines: Agents check the determinable: schema, timing, paths, resolution.
Humans check the judgeable: interesting, worth watching, publish.
Gates are cheap taste-checks before expensive ones.
The determinism boundary is the human-judgment boundary.

beats.narration_text: When should we use AI here? Use it where a mistake can be named, tested, and corrected: missing files, broken timing, inconsistent structure. Do not use it to surrender the question of meaning: whether the work is honest, worthwhile, or ready to meet another person. The boundary is not a failure of intelligence. It is where responsibility begins.

props.artifactTitle: When to use AI — and when not to
artifactLines: USE AI — explicit evidence · reversible checks · repeatable rules
DO NOT DELEGATE — meaning · fairness · worth · publication
The machine reports. A person remains answerable.

beats.narration_text: Follow the question further at Medhavy.com.
